plainMeaning: I bow to the soft, tender feet of those great teachers — feet bearing auspicious marks and revered by hundreds of their disciples.
recitation: sukumāla komala tale, tesiṁ paṇāmāmi lakkhaṇa pasatthe. pāe pāvayaṇīṇaṁ, paḍicchaga saehiṁ paṇivaie.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. su-kumāra (su- 'well, very' + kumāra 'tender, youthful'); Prakrit sukumāla.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies tale (soles); paired with komala as a near-synonym doublet.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. komala 'soft, tender'; unchanged in Prakrit.
wordByWord.notes: Forms an intensifying pair with sukumāla — 'delicate-and-soft'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tala 'surface, sole'; here loc./acc. linking to pāe.
wordByWord.notes: The under-surface of the feet; the precise locus of the homage.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. teṣāṁ (gen. pl. of tad); Prakrit tesiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Refers back to the virtue-laden teachers catalogued in the preceding verse (v.24).
The main verb of the sentence; first person singular present.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. lakṣaṇa 'mark, characteristic'; Prakrit lakkhaṇa (kṣ → kkh).
wordByWord.notes: Compounds with pasatthe — the praiseworthy bodily signs (lotus, etc.) of great souls.
Agrees with tale/pāe; 'graced with praiseworthy marks'.
Object of paṇāmāmi; expands the image from tale (soles) to the whole feet.

wordByWord.gloss: of the masters of the doctrine (pravacanins)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. prāvacanika / pravacanin (from pravacana 'authoritative exposition'); gen. pl. Prakrit pāvayaṇīṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Those authorized to expound the canon — synonymous in force with vācaka.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pratīcchaka 'one who receives/accepts' (prati + √iṣ); Prakrit paḍicchaga.
wordByWord.notes: Forms a compound with saehiṁ — 'by hundreds of disciples'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śataiḥ (instr. pl. of śata 'hundred'); Prakrit saehiṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Instrumental of agent with the participle paṇivaie; hyperbole for a vast disciple-community.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. praṇipatita 'prostrated before' (pra + ni + √pat); Prakrit paṇivaie (past participle).
wordByWord.notes: Passive participle qualifying pāe — the feet 'that are prostrated before' by the disciples.

literalTranslation: Their soft, tender soles, graced with praiseworthy marks — I bow to them; the feet of the masters of the doctrine, feet prostrated before by hundreds of disciples.
idiomaticTranslation: To their delicate, soft soles — adorned with the auspicious marks of greatness — I bow low; the feet of those expounders of the holy doctrine, feet that hundreds of disciples reverently fall before.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse is the grammatical and devotional climax of the homage begun in the previous gāthā. Verse 24 piles up a long genitive string of virtues — austerity, restraint, truth, self-control, humility, forbearance, the foremost of the age in anuyoga — but supplies no main verb. Here that suspended praise finally lands: tesiṁ paṇāmāmi, 'to them I bow.' The object is deliberately concrete and humble — not the teachers' fame or learning, but their feet (pāe), indeed the very soles (tale). The poet dwells on those soles as sukumāla komala, 'delicate and soft': despite a lifetime of barefoot vihāra, the feet of these great souls remain tender — a tradition reading their physical unblemishedness as a sign of inner purity and ahiṁsā. They are lakkhaṇa-pasattha, marked with the auspicious lakṣaṇas (lotus and the like) borne by great beings. The second line widens the lens: these are the feet of the pāvayaṇī — the authorized expounders of the canon — and they are paḍicchaga-saehiṁ paṇivaie, 'prostrated before by hundreds of disciples.' That single phrase quietly evidences the lineage's living vitality: each master stands amid a crowd of receivers (paḍicchaga) who carry the teaching onward. Having completed this collective bow to the yuga-pradhāna masters, the hymn is poised to turn, in the next verse, to all the remaining guardians of the Kālika anuyoga and to announce that the exposition of knowledge itself now begins.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse enacts pāda-vandanā — homage rendered specifically at the guru's feet — the bodily idiom of vinaya (reverential humility) that Jain teaching makes the indispensable gateway to jñāna. Bowing precedes learning: one earns the canon by first lowering oneself before those who hold it. The 'soft soles graced with praiseworthy marks' transpose the mahāpuruṣa-lakṣaṇa imagery, usually reserved for the Tīrthaṅkaras, onto the human teacher-lineage, dignifying the ācāryas as worthy intermediaries of the Jina's word. The detail paḍicchaga-saehiṁ frames transmission as relational: knowledge survives only where receivers gather to accept it, the very dynamic this Sthavirāvalī commemorates.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As the reciter reaches this verse, the bow becomes interior: one mentally prostrates at the feet of every teacher in the chain that carried the scripture down to today. The lesson is to approach study itself with bowed-head humility — vinaya before vidyā — and to recognize oneself as one more paḍicchaga, a receiver duty-bound to keep the teaching alive for the next hundred.

notes: tesiṁ ('of them') is anaphoric, pointing back to the virtue-laden teachers enumerated in v.24; vv.24–25 form one syntactic unit (genitive epithets in v.24, main verb paṇāmāmi here). saehiṁ is read as śataiḥ ('by hundreds') compounded with paḍicchaga ('disciples'); a bare instrumental of the demonstrative is theoretically possible but the disciple-count reading fits the lineage context decisively. lakkhaṇa-pasatthe is taken with the feet/soles ('marked with auspicious signs'). The verse text is reproduced verbatim from the book (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.); every mātrā, anusvāra and conjunct follows the source and has not been normalized. One source URL (jainelibrary.org) is a portal root rather than a deep link to this specific gāthā.
